coredata.dc:title: Crystal structures, DFT and fluorimetric study of a dual nature Schiff base chemosensor for detection of Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions in aqueous media
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahbaz, Muhammad
Sharif, Shahzad
Malik, Fouzia Perveen
Shah, Javed Hussain
Arooj, Anum
Şahin, Onur
Riaz, Bilal
Dar, Birra
Khan, Islam Ullah
coredata.dc:description: A dual nature Schiff base chemosensor“3,5-bis(((2-hydroxynaphthalen-1-yl)methylene)amino) benzoic acid” (3,5-BHNMABA) was synthesized and characterized by single crystal X-rays diffraction. The interaction of the photophysical properties of the ligand with various metal ions (aqueous) was extensively analyzed through fluorescence spectrophotometry. EHOMO-ELUMO energy gap and molecular electrostatic potential study was performed by DFT calculations. Strong quenching in emission was observed for Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions. Binding ratio between Cu2+/Ce4+ ions with ligand was calculated by Job’s plot. LOD and LOQ were calculated as 7.92 × 10-6 M and 2.40 × 10-5 M for Cu2+ ions whereas 5.9 × 10-6 M and 1.97 × 10-5 M for Ce4+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Schiff base
X-ray crystal structures
Turn-off fluorescent
Job’s plot
Cu2+ ions
Ce4+ ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Crystallographic study of 3,5-BHNMABA and Cu-3,5-BHNMABA complex
Determination of limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) for Ce4+ and Cu2+ metal ions
Determination of Stern-Volmer Constants and Job’s plot for Ce4+ and Cu2+ metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)The molecular structure of Cu-3,5-BHNMABA. (B)The Cu(II)···Cu(II) separation in Cu-3,5-BHNMABA.

(A) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra. (B) UV–Visible absorption spectra of 3,5-BHNMABA and Cu-3,5-BHNMABA complex.(C) Fluorescent emission spectra and (D) bar graph of the sensor in the presence of different metal ions.

(A) Quenching graph of the sensor with Ce4+ ions and (B) graphical representation. (C) Quenching graph of the sensor with Cu2+ ions and (D) graphical representation. (E) LOD/LOQ determination of Ce4+ ions and (F) Cu2+ ions.

(A)Stern-Volmer graph for quenching of ligand 3,5-BHNMABA in the presence of different concentrations of Ce4+ ions and (B) Cu2+ ions. (λex = 288 nm, λem = 348 nm). (C)Photo-inducted electron transfer (PET) mechanism. (D) Illustration of quenching mechanism of fluorescence of sensor by Ce4+ and Cu2+ ions.(E) Job’s plot experiment of 3,5-BHNMABA with Cu2+ ions by plotting a graph between X*(Io-I) and [Cu2+]/[ Cu2+] + [3,5-BHNMABA]. (F)Job’s plot experiment of 3,5-BHNMABA with Ce4+ ions by plotting graph between X*(Io-I) and [Ce4+]/[Ce4+] + [3,5-BHNMABA].(G) Effect of pH on the fluorescent intensity of 3,5-BHNMABA.
(a) Frontier molecular orbital analysis and distribution of iso-densities indicating regions of reactivity. (b) Optimized geometry of the 3,5-BHNMABA.
Quenching behavior of emission intensity in real samples.
Crystal data and structure refinement parameters for5-BHNMABA and Cu-3,5-BHNMABA.
Quantitative recovery percentage of Cu(II) and Ce(IV) ions from various spiked water samples.
Limit of Detection (LOD) of various ligands for Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions.

head.ce:title: Crystal structures, DFT and fluorimetric study of a dual nature Schiff base chemosensor for detection of Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions in aqueous media

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual nature Schiff base chemosensor“3,5-bis(((2-hydroxynaphthalen-1-yl)methylene)amino) benzoic acid” (3,5-BHNMABA) was synthesized and characterized by single crystal X-rays diffraction. The interaction of the photophysical properties of the ligand with various metal ions (aqueous) was extensively analyzed through fluorescence spectrophotometry. EHOMO-ELUMO energy gap and molecular electrostatic potential study was performed by DFT calculations. Strong quenching in emission was observed for Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions. Binding ratio between Cu2+/Ce4+ ions with ligand was calculated by Job’s plot. LOD and LOQ were calculated as 7.92 × 10-6 M and 2.40 × 10-5 M for Cu2+ ions whereas 5.9 × 10-6 M and 1.97 × 10-5 M for Ce4+ ions.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the existence of non-metal anions, various metal ions (like iron, copper, cobalt, and cerium) are frequently encountered in our daily lives. No doubt, certain metals belonging to the ‘s’ and ‘d’ blocks of the periodic table are categorized as essential metals for their significant role played in the biological area but the quantity of such metals crossing the permissible limit poses severe toxic effects to animals and plants [1]. For instance, copper being third most important transition metal in human body plays a dynamic role in pathological and physiological processes such as antioxidant defense, respiration, gene expression, enzyme functions, blood cell formation, iron metabolism, signal transduction and free radical detoxification [2]. Moreover, it also maintains the growth of brain tissues, heart, kidneys, and other body organs while its metalcomplexes can kill cancer cells without having any adverse effect on normal cells [3]. The role of copper as a cofactor is significant due to electrical conductivity, good ductility, and redox properties [4].Copper metal is widely used in industrial catalytic chemical reactions [5–7]. Despite all the benefits, any imbalance in the supply of Cu2+ions can cause severe hazards. It may be responsible for several neurodegenerative diseases [8], pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), Hashimoto’s disease, stomach cramps, breast diseases, hematological manifestation, liver and kidney damage, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and prion diseases [9]. Copper deficiency may lead to hair abnormalities, anemia, false development of brain and bones, and arterial abnormalities [4]. At the same time, the overuse of Cu2+ions may cause the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [10].Copper ions are also considered as the main environmental pollutant that circulates in our environment due to their excessive use in industries and agriculture. For adults, the lethal copper dose is between 4 and 400 mg/kg of body weight [11]. According to the “World Health Organization” (WHO), the concentration of copper ions in drinking water and in human blood should not exceed 31.5 µM (2 ppm) and 15.7 µM − 23.6µ respectively [12].

Rare earth elements (REEs) possess a broad spectrum of applications in advanced technological domains because of their special physical and chemical properties. They are used in optics, agriculture, catalysis, magnetism, glass, ceramics, electronics, nuclear radiation, chemical industry, and medicines. Cerium is most abundant among lanthanides with the crustal abundance of 66 ppm [13,14]. Cerium is a very crucial oxidant and possesses theability to interact with different biological molecules like DNA and proteins [15]. Cerium is used in diesel fuel, catalytic converter in vehicles, and fertilizers. In the realm of metallurgy, cerium plays an important role in aluminum alloys and stainless steel as a catalyst for precipitation hardening. In steels, cerium is also used to degasifies and mitigate the sulfides and oxides [16]. The radioactive cerium-137 is themost dangerous substance which was released during the Fukushima nuclear accident with an extendable half-life of about 30.2 years. Radioactive cerium is one of the most emitted products of uranium fission reactions from nuclear reactors and around 3.3 × 1016 Bq of cerium is being released into environments. Cerium causes severe health problems in biological systems, cardiovascular diseases and gastrointestinal distress, sensitivity to heat, abnormal blood, acute myocardial closure, biochemical indexes, leukemia, and skin lesions [17,18]. Cerium is linked with the production of cancerous line cells in the colon and liver and also damages cell membranes, and reproductive as well as nervous systems [4,13].

Consequently, the researchers are focusing on the development of physio-chemical and biological techniques for measurement of copper and cerium ions in aqueous media. Undoubtedly, the industrial revolution has reshaped our lifestyle to the next level but it has also damaged our biological system as the availability of clean water is a major concern for sustainable human life. This need for survival has persuaded us to explore new facile and economic techniques for the quantitative and qualitative detection of toxic metal ions in aqueous media. The existing approaches for detection includes potentiometric method, AAS, XRF, anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV), potentiometric probes based on ion-selective electrodes and mass spectrometry [13,19]. Nonetheless, these methods offer certain limitations like high cost, impracticality, and time-consumption [18,20]. There is an escalating need for sensor technology with widespread advantages of environmental friendly, high selectivity and sensitivity, affordable, uncomplicated, fast response and portable fluorescent sensor for detection of heavy metals in water-based systems.

In contrast, colorimetric and fluorimetric techniques grabbed much attention due to sensitivity, selectivity and rapid response [21]. Several fluorescent chemosensors have been developed to detect metal [22].Chemosensor 3,5-BHNMABA has already been synthesized for detection of Cu2+and Ce4+ ions [3]. However, crystal study, computational analysis, and many spectrophotometric applications were yet to be looked over.

In this work, a naphthalene-based compound 3,5-BHNMABA was synthesized by condensation reaction with slight medication in the reported method [3]. Single crystal XRD analysis was carried out to unearth the real structures of the ligand as well as ligand complex which drew a real pen picture of binding mode of 3,5-BHNMABA with metal ions. Apart from in-depth structural and fluorimetric re-evaluation of the sensing behavior of 3,5-BHNMABA towards copper ions, the area of study was extended to lanthanides such as cerium ions. Previously, binding of the probe with Cu2+ ions was proposed only but it was not explored to the length of practical confirmation. Cu-3,5-BHNMABA complex was prepared and characterized by single crystal XRD which showed different binding behavior and it can guide towards a new direction for further practical applications. When the ligand solution in dimethyl formamide (DMF)/H2O was treated with aqueous solution of different metal ions (Cr3+,Ce4+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Tb3+, Er3+, Nd3+, Ni2+, Ag2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Gd3+, Co2+,Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Al3+) a turn-off response was observed with Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions. LOD, LOQ limit of detection and quantification, Stern-Volmer constant and stoichiometric ratio were studied in length and it was found that the ligand served as an efficient tool for the detection of Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions.

Absolute ethanol (solvent), the precursor reagents (3,5-diaminobenzoic acid and 2-hydroxy-1-naphthaldehyde; 96 % purity), and Nitrate/Chloride salts of the selected metal ions like Cr3+,Ce4+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Tb3+, Er3+, Nd3+, Ni2+, Ag2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Gd3+, Co2+,Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+and Al3+ were procured commercially. The stock solution of selected metal ions with a concentration of 1 × 10-2 M was prepared in deionized water. Characterization ofthe ligandwas made by FT-IR and single-crystal XRD analysis. The fluorescence study ofligand was carried out in a quartz cuvette using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence spectrophotometer. Excitation and emission was observed by adjusting the slit width at 2 nm. Medium scan control at scan rate of 600 nm/min with average time of 0.1 s having data interval of 1 nm was used.

For the preparation of homogeneous solutions ofthe ligand and different metal ions, Elma E 30H Elmasonic (sonicator) was used. For reliability and reproducibility, the experiment was repeated thrice.Crystal structure determination and refinement parameters are presented in supplementary material.

Separate ethanolic solutions of 3,5-diaminobenzoic acid (2) (1 mmol,) and 2-hydroxy-1-naphthaldehyde (3) (2 mmol, g) in 10 ml were prepared in the round bottom flask containing magnetic stirrer. The reaction mixture was refluxed at 80 °C with continuous stirring for 8 h ( Scheme 1
). Orange color precipitates were obtained which were filtered, washed with water, dried [3], and recrystallized by using DMF. Yield = 75 %. Appearance: Orange color solid, Melting point: 300 °C, Molecular Formula: C29H18N2O4, Molecular Weight: 458 g/mol. FTIR spectra is presented is Fig. S1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallographic study of 3,5-BHNMABA and Cu-3,5-BHNMABA complex
ce:section.ce:para:
The molecular structure of 3,5-BHNMABA is shown in Fig. 1
. The O2-C18 and O5-C30 bond distances are typical double bonds [1.194(3) Å and 1.211(4) Å]. The dihedral angles between phenyl and naphthalene rings are 5.27(2)° and 7.49(2)°. The O3 atom acts as a H-bond donor, via atom H3A, to atom O4 in the molecule at (2-x, 2-y, 1-z), forming R2 2(22) rings centered at (1, 1, 1/2). O-H···O and C-H···O hydrogen bonds produce S(6), R2 1(7), and R3 3(9) rings, bond lengths, and angles and Hydrogen bond parameters are given in Table S1and S2. Compound 1 also contains two π···π interactions between the naphthalene rings of neighbouring molecules ( Table S3 ). The distances between the ring centroids are 3.532 (3) Å and 3.780 (3) Å.

Cu-3,5-BHNMABA complex ( Fig. 2
A) was prepared with 64 % yield, m.p was observed at 395 °C.FTIR spectra is presented is Fig. S2 . The asymmetric unit of Cu-3,5-BHNMABA consists of one Cu(II) ion, one 3,5-BHNMABA, one non-coordinated dimethylformamide molecule, and one non-coordinated 2-methylprop-1-ene-1,1-diol molecule. The Cu(II) ion is coordinated by two nitrogen atoms [Cu1-N1 = 1.964(5) Å and Cu1-N2i = 1.962(5) Å] and two oxygen atoms [Cu1-O1 = 1.903(5) Å and Cu1-O2i = 1.901(4) Å] from 3,5-BHNMABA. Details of data collection and crystal structure determinations are given in Table 1 for 3,5-BHNMABA and Cu-3,5-BHNMABA. The Cu(II) ions are bridged by 3,5-BHNMABA ligand ( Fig. 2
B) with the Cu(II)···Cu(II) separation is 7.434 Å, hydrogen bond parameters are given in Table S4.

The fluorescence spectroscopy technique was used to find the sensing ability of 3,5-BHNMABA towards different metal ions. A solution of ligand in DMF/H2O (1 × 10-2 M) was prepared. The stock solutions (1 × 10-4 M) of various metal ions (Cr3+,Ce4+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Tb3+, Er3+, Nd3+, Ni2+, Ag2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Gd3+, Co2+,Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+and Al3+) was prepared in deionized water. The ligand showed excitation at 288 nm and emission at 348 nm ( Fig. 3
A). Excitation and emission slit widths were adjusted at 2.0 nm. UV–visible absorption spectra also confirmed qualitative behavior of 3,5-BHNMABA towards Cu (II) ion in solution ( Fig. 3
B). When the ligand solution was treated with an aqueous solution of different metal ions, the emission intensity was quenched remarkably by the solution of Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions ( Fig. 3
C), while graphical representation is presented in Fig. 3
D. After 5 s of addition of metal ions precise stable results was obtained.Cu2+ ions interact with 3,5-BHNMABA forming a stable complex which is responsible for turn-off behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) for Ce4+ and Cu2+ metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing behavior of 3,5-BHNMABA towards Ce4+ and Cu2+ metal ions was also examined by titration method. Upon gradual increase in the concentration of Ce4+(1x10-4 M) ions into the ligand,fluorescence intensity of the ligand disappeared completely with an excellent linear relationship. Upon each addition of the metal ionsfluorimetric response thereof was determined. Fluorescence intensity of the ligand solution was quenched as a function of the concentration of metal ions (Ce4+ and Cu2+ ions). A linear response was recorded with the value of R2 as 0.9915 and 0.9956 for Ce4+ and Cu2+ ions respectively ( Fig. 4
B & 4D). LOD) and LOQ for Ce4+ and Cu2+ ions can be determined with the help of graphs Fig. 4
E & 4F by using equation-1 & 2[23] as follows:
(1)
LOD = 3.3 × S D o f i n t e r c e p t / s l o p e
(2)
LOQ = 10 × S D o f i n t e r c e p t / s l o p e
where SD is standard deviation and slope relates to the quenching graph. LOD and LOQ for Cu (II) ions were 7.2 × 10-6M and 2.4 × 10-5M for Ce (IV) ions came out as 5.9 × 10-6 M and 1.9 × 10-5M respectively. Cu (II) ions show strong fluorescence property and also undergo electronic transitions as well resulting into strong emission singles. On the other hands, Ce (IV) ions having weaker fluorescence coupled with unfavorable electronic transition. Moreover, the strong complexing ability and environmental stability of Cu (II) ions with ligands results into higher LOD and LOQ as compared with Ce (IV) ions [24,25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Stern-Volmer Constants and Job’s plot for Ce4+ and Cu2+ metal ions
ce:section.ce:para:
A quencher is a substance which decreases fluorescence intensity of the ligand. There are two well-known categories of fluorescence quenching; (i) dynamic quenching which is observed when a quencher interacts with excited substance via diffusion and in (ii) static quenching, quencher is combined with fluorophore to form non-fluorescent complex. Stern-Volmer plot is commonly used to study nature of quenching mechanisms using equation-3 as follows:
(3)
I o
/ I
= 1 +
K
sv
Q

Static quenching;
(4)
Io / I
=
1 +
K s
Q

Dynamic quenching;
(5)
Io / I
=
1 +
K D
Q

Static and dynamic quenching;
(6)
Io / I
=
1 +
K s
Q
1 +
K D
Q

Where I0 is fluorescence intensity of the ligand solution without metal ions, I is fluorescence intensity of the ligand in presence of metal ions, KD = kq × to, Q represents the concentration of metal ions in the ligand solution, Ksv denotes the Stern Volmer Constant. to is lifetime of the emissive excited state and kq is quenching rate coefficient [22]. Polynomial cures ( Fig. 5
A & 4B) between I0/I and [Q] were obtained which confirmed dynamic as well as static quenching. Stable complex was formed by the fluorescent molecule due to thephoto-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism ( Fig. 5
C) which is based on interaction of electron donating unit and fluorophoric acceptor moiety combined via linker. The reduction potential is increased in ionophore HOMO in result of guest binding, having moderate energy as compared to fluorophore HOMO. Due to donation of electron from ionophore to guest units, the electron from half-filled LUMO of fluorophore transfer to HOMO of fluorophore which results into PET-OFF. No significant spectral shift is sheen in emission wavelength and the interaction of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) as well as lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of the sensor with the LUMO of the metal ions results into formation of stable metal–ligand complex and non-radiative path is followed ( Fig. 5
D). On the basis of quenching experimental data, equation-3 was used to determine Stern-Volmerconstant(Ksv) for Ce4+ ions (6.09 × 104 M−1) and Cu2+ ions (6. 93 × 104 M−1).

To determine the stoichiometry between metal ions and ligand molecules, Job’s plot experiment was performed. Same concentration of 3,5-BHNMABA and Ce4+/Cu2+ ions (1x10-6 M) were used to draw a graph was between X*(Io-I) and [Ce4+]/[ Ce4+] + [3,5-BHNMABA] ( Fig. 5
E&5F). Where X is mole fraction, Io is fluorescence intensity of sensor without adding analyte and I represent the intensity of various concentrations of Ce4+/Cu2+ ions. The experiment showed the binding ratio of 1:1 between metal ions and ligand molecules.Furthermore, the effect of pH on the fluorescent intensity was also investigated. The sensor solution showed slight acidic pH-about 6.7 and changing the pH from 1 to 6 and 8 to 12 decreased the fluorescent intensity of the sensor solution.

FMOs calculations were performed using DFT. The Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO), signifies the electron-donating capability, while the lower-energy frontier molecular orbital (LUMO), signifies the electron-accepting capacity [15]. The corresponding EHOMO and ELUMO values were found to be are −6.36 eV and −4.96 eV ( Fig. 6
a). The energy gap between the EHOMO-ELUMO gap i.e; ΔE for the compound was found to be 1.4 eV. It is evident from these values that electron transfer in 3,5-BHNMABA is very efficient due to its smaller EHOMO-ELUMO gap and hence it can act as proficient sensing material. The smaller value of the bandgap also depicted greater reactivity and polarizability of the molecule. FMO analysis also helps in depicting iso-densities of HOMO and LUMO surfaces to understand electronic distribution. Highest distribution of iso-densitiesis was on heteroatoms. In HOMO iso-densities are distributed over naphthalene ring, hence it acts as electron donor, whereas LUMO iso-densities are distributed on the Cu atom acting as electron acceptors indicating that Cu ions accept electrons from naphthalene ring. The turn off intensity of the ligand is considered due to photo-inducted electron transfer (PET) mechanism. In case of Ce (IV), it seems that dominant quenching mechanism is dynamic in nature due to tunneling of electron excitation from excited Ce (IV) ions [26].

Bond properties analysis was carried to estimate nature of bonding between 3,5-BHNMABAand Cu ions when being removed from water and has been represented in the Fig. 6
a. It is apparent that Cu is able to make with two −O (B.L = 5.018Ǻ, 3.317Ǻ) and with two N atoms (3.76Ǻ, 2.079Ǻ) indicating that Cu did not develop covalent bond with 3,5-BHNMABA acid and hence its surface is able to be refreshed very easily after removal from the water.

Optimized geometry of the compound was utilized to map MESP to determine electrophilic and nucleophilic character of the compound (Fig. 6 b) which reveals that negative potential of the 3,5-BHNMABA is in the form of blue mesh is concentrated on oxygen, nitrogen and electron efficient naphthalene group which reflects electron transfer from these regions to Cu ion. yellow color indicated that H-atoms Whereas Cu ion contribute as electrophilic region.

The real sample analysis was performed in four samples as given in Table 2
. 100 μL of 100 μM concentration of sensor was added in each filtered 3.0 mL samples. Fluorescence emission intensity was measured before and after spiking of 20 μM of Cu2+ and Ce4+( Fig. 7
). The % age recoveries are presented in (
Table 2 ). Moreover, detail of physical appearance, pH, TDS and viscosity is given in Table S5 . Suitability of the sensor for detecting Cu2+ and Ce4+ ions in real samples is well revealed on the basis of % age recoveries is given in Table 2, while comparison of this work with previous literature is presented in Table 3 .

In this report, we have reported a naphthalene-based ligand (3,5-BHNMABA) for efficient and selective sensing of toxic Cu2+and Ce4+ ions present in aqueous medium. Synthesized ligand and its copper complex were characterized by single crystal XRD. The fluorescent intensity of the ligand solution (DMF/Water) was quenched remarkably when aqueous solution of Ce4+ and Cu2+ ions was added. Stoichiometry of 1:1 between metal and ligand was determined by Job’s plot and further confirmed by getting XRD analysis of the Cu-BHNMAB complex which drew a different picture of bonding as compared to earlier report proposed bonding. Static as well as dynamic quenching mechanisms were supported by Stern-Volmer plot and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) resulted in quenching. LOD and LOQ of the potential fluorescence probe were observed as 7.92 × 10-6M and 2.40 × 10-5M for Cu2+ ions whereas 5.9 × 10-6 M and 1.97 × 10-5 M for Ce4+ ions respectively. Small EHOMO-ELUMO energy gap of 1.4 eV was calculated by DFT revealed efficient sensing of synthesized material. To conclude, it is proposed that the chemosensor (3,5-BHNMABA) has advantage over the various reported chemosensor as it can be used as a dual function potential turn-off fluorescence sensor for the detection of copper as well as cerium ions in aqueous medium.
Ethical Approval: Not applicable.
Funding: There was no funding to disclose.

Muhammad Shahbaz: Writing – original draft. Shahzad Sharif: Resources, Project administration. Fouzia Perveen Malik: Software. Javed Hussain Shah: Methodology. Anum Arooj: Formal analysis. Onur Şahin: Validation. Bilal Riaz: Data curation. Birra Dar: Investigation. Islam Ullah Khan: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial assistance from the Higher Education Commission of Pakistan, HEC-NRPU project no. 20-17612/NRPU/R&D/HEC/2021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.113666.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of nanoparticle doping on the colloidal stability and toxicity of copper oxide nanoparticles in synthetic and natural waters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adeleye, A. S.; Pokhrel, S.; Mädler, L.; Keller, A. A. Influence of nanoparticle doping on the colloidal stability and toxicity of copper oxide nanoparticles in synthetic and natural waters. Water Res.2018, 132, 12–22. Doi: 10.1016/j.watres.2017.12.069.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of burning of fireworks on particle size distribution of PM10 and associated Barium at Nagpur

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khaparde, V. V.; Pipalatkar, P. P.; Pustode, T.; Rao, C. V. C.; Gajghate, D. G. Influence of burning of fireworks on particle size distribution of PM10 and associated Barium at Nagpur. Environ. Monit. Assess.2012, 184 (2), 903–911. Doi: 10.1007/s10661-011-2008-8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective detection of copper ion in water by tetradentate ligand sensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: ReddyPrasad, P.; Imae, T. Selective detection of copper ion in water by tetradentate ligand sensor. J. Taiwan Inst. Chem. Eng.2017, 72, 194–199. Doi: 10.1016/j.jtice.2017.01.009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrated miniature fluorescent probe to leverage the sensing potential of ZnO quantum dots for the detection of copper (II) ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ng, S. M.; Wong, D. S. N.; Phung, J. H. C.; Chua, H. S. Integrated miniature fluorescent probe to leverage the sensing potential of ZnO quantum dots for the detection of copper (II) ions. Talanta2013, 116, 514–519. Doi: 10.1016/j.talanta.2013.07.031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxidation of phenyl propyne catalyzed by copper(II) complexes of a benzimidazolyl schiff base ligand: effect of acid/base, oxidant, surfactant and morphology

sb:maintitle.ce:italic: Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
